notes: baseline SORT config ported from sort.ipynb, unmodified — establishes the starting point for the autoresearch loop.
baseline SORT config ported from sort.ipynb, unmodified — establishes the starting point for the autoresearch loop.

Hypothesis: GT checkpoints are recorded at the exact frame each instrument is first introduced (verified directly: in IMG_2044, 13/14 checkpoints coincide with a brand-new track_id appearing). With minimum_consecutive_frames=60 (2s), a diagnostic showed 25-of-29 misses on IMG_2044 (and the same dominant pattern on every other clip) were 'immature_spawn': a confident, well-localized (IoU>=0.3) detection existed in the tracker's raw output but was still tagged tracker_id=-1 because its tracklet hadn't yet accumulated 60 consecutive updates -- i.e. the 60-frame maturity gate all but guarantees a miss at an instrument's own introduction checkpoint, independent of tracking quality. SORTTracklet starts number_of_successful_updates at 1 on spawn (SORT-paper convention), so minimum_consecutive_frames=1 lets a track earn its ID on the very frame it's detected, which should directly target this failure mode without touching lost_track_buffer (already generous at 20s, so re-association after brief occlusion of an already-mature track shouldn't be affected by this change).

Follow-up to the minimum_consecutive_frames=1 win (0.244->0.109 agg, 0.295->0.180 holdout): that fix traded misses for switches (e.g. IMG_2081 switches 1->7), because a track that drops its detection for even one frame under occlusion/fast hand motion and gets Kalman-drift-mismatched on reappearance now spawns a brand-new track that *immediately* earns an ID instead of quietly sitting immature. Hypothesis: OC-SORT's whole design target is exactly this failure mode -- observation-centric re-update (ORU) replays a virtual linear trajectory from last-observation to new-observation on reacquisition instead of trusting Kalman's occlusion-time drift, and OCR (2nd-stage association against each lost track's last real observation, not its drifted prediction) plus direction-consistency should let the *original* tracklet reclaim its detection instead of losing it to a fresh spawn, cutting switches without reintroducing the maturity-gate miss (kept minimum_consecutive_frames=1). NOTE: first attempt used XCYCSRStateEstimator (OC-SORT's own default) and crashed with a NaN from `sqrt` of a negative interpolated scale inside ORU's virtual-trajectory replay (`_unfreeze_xcycsr`) -- a real edge case in that library combination, apparently triggered by our very large lost_track_buffer (20s) letting tracks accumulate a long-enough gap for the scale interpolation to go degenerate. Switched to XYXYStateEstimator (corner-based, no scale/sqrt term) to sidestep it, since it's the one lever available without touching the `trackers` package itself.

Follow-up to minimum_consecutive_frames=1 (best so far: 0.109 agg / 0.180 holdout). Now that misses are cheap to recover from (a re-spawned track matures instantly), switches/merges are the dominant remaining error, and those are association-quality problems, not maturity-gate problems. Hypothesis: minimum_iou_threshold=-0.30 on GIoU is permissive enough to accept matches between boxes that don't even overlap (GIoU is negative there) -- on a cluttered tray with several similarly-sized steel instruments close together, that's exactly the setting that lets a track's Kalman-predicted box glom onto a *neighboring* instrument's detection instead of correctly going unmatched, producing both switches (track drifts onto a different GT identity) and merges (two tracks nearby end up trading a detection back and forth). Raising the threshold to 0.10 (requiring genuine, if small, overlap) should reject those cross-instrument grabs; since lost_track_buffer is generous and re-maturation is now instant, a track that goes briefly unmatched because of the stricter threshold has multiple future frames to reacquire the correct detection instead of permanently losing it to a neighbor.

Follow-up to minimum_iou_threshold=0.10 (best so far: 0.074 agg / 0.128 holdout, down from -0.30's 0.109/0.180). That confirmed tightening the association gate helps -- pushing further to 0.30 (matching eval.py's own IOU_MATCH_THRESHOLD, i.e. requiring an association at least as strict as what counts as a correct match for scoring) to see whether the trend continues or whether 0.10 already captured most of the benefit and further tightening starts trading merges/switches back for misses (stricter gate -> more frames where a genuinely-occluded-but-still-nearby detection gets rejected -> track goes unmatched -> eventually counted as a miss instead of a bad association).

Bisecting the minimum_iou_threshold sweep: 0.10 gave 0.074 agg / 0.128 holdout (best so far); pushing to 0.30 made aggregate worse (0.084) while holdout improved (0.115) -- merges vanished but switches rose, i.e. 0.30 overcorrects. Trying 0.20 to see if it lands between the two on both metrics, or whether the aggregate optimum really is nearer 0.10 and 0.30's holdout improvement was more coincidence (IMG_2112-specific) than a genuine generalizing trend.

0.20 beat both 0.10 (0.074/0.128) and 0.30 (0.084/0.115) on *both* metrics (0.070 agg / 0.103 holdout) -- a real sweet spot, not a monotonic trend, so narrowing further: trying 0.25 to see which side of 0.20 the true optimum sits on before declaring it found.

0.20 is the best point found so far (0.070 agg / 0.103 holdout); 0.25 already regressed (0.079 agg). Checking the other side of the sweet spot with 0.15 to confirm 0.20 is a genuine local optimum in both directions rather than the sweep just having stepped over a still-better value between 0.10 and 0.20.

0.15 improved again over 0.20 (0.067 agg, holdout tied at 0.103) -- sweep so far: 0.10=0.074, 0.15=0.067, 0.20=0.070, 0.25=0.079, 0.30=0.084, forming a shallow bowl centered near 0.15. Trying 0.12 to see if the true minimum is between 0.10 and 0.15, or whether 0.15 is close enough to the bottom that this is now just noise from a handful of clip-specific switch/merge trade-offs.

minimum_iou_threshold sweep plateaued at 0.15 (best: 0.067 agg / 0.103 holdout); moving to a different lever now: the IoU variant itself. Hypothesis: surgical instruments (scalpels, forceps, scissors) are rigid and strongly elongated, holding a roughly constant aspect ratio while they slide/rotate slightly under hand manipulation. GIoU's only non-overlap signal is the enclosing-box gap fraction, which doesn't care about shape at all -- a track could glom onto a same-sized-but-differently-oriented neighboring instrument's box just as easily as the correct one. CIoU adds an explicit aspect-ratio-mismatch penalty (via the arctan(w/h) term) on top of GIoU's center-distance-style penalty, which should specifically discourage exactly that kind of cross-instrument mismatch on a cluttered tray. Kept minimum_iou_threshold=0.15 to isolate the IoU-variant swap as the only change from the current best.

minimum_iou_threshold sweep plateaued at 0.15 (best: 0.067 agg / 0.103 holdout); moving to a different lever now: the IoU variant itself. 0.20 is the best point found so far (0.070 agg / 0.103 holdout); 0.25 already regressed (0.079 agg). Checking the other side of the sweet spot with 0.15 to confirm 0.20 is a genuine local optimum in both directions rather than the sweep just having stepped over a still-better value between 0.10 and 0.20.

CIoU@0.15 beat GIoU@0.15 on train aggregate (0.058 vs 0.067) but regressed holdout badly (0.128 vs 0.103), and the regression didn't go away at other thresholds either (0.10: 0.141 holdout, 0.20: 0.103 holdout, i.e. only tying, never beating, GIoU). Traced a concrete mechanism in IMG_2047: a 'long metal bone punch' (gt=5) has a huge diagonal bbox that geometrically overlaps a nearby 'thin metal pin' (gt=3)'s bbox in axis-aligned coordinates even though they don't visually overlap -- exactly the kind of ambiguity an aspect-ratio-aware penalty like CIoU's should help resolve (thin-tall vs wide-flat boxes look different). But CIoU's aspect-ratio term operates on axis-aligned w/h, which changes when a *rigid* instrument is simply rotated on the tray during handling -- a legitimate pose change that CIoU would penalize as if the object's shape itself changed. That's a plausible reason it hurts generalization: IMG_2112 (holdout) may just have more mid-handling rotation than the aspect-ratio-stable train clips. DIoU keeps CIoU's other idea (penalize association across a large center-to-center gap, which the bone-punch/pin case also exhibits -- their centers are ~1300px apart) while dropping the rotation-sensitive aspect-ratio term, which should be more robust to instrument rotation than CIoU without giving up GIoU's plain enclosing-box-gap baseline.

IoU-variant detour (CIoU, DIoU) both improved train aggregate but consistently regressed holdout -- reverted to GIoU@0.15 (0.067 agg / 0.103 holdout) as the best generalizing config, now trying a different lever. Hypothesis: track_activation_threshold=0.90 sits above CONFIDENCE_THRESHOLD=0.80, so any detection scored 0.80-0.90 that fails to match an existing track passes through the tracker but is blocked from spawning a *new* track -- it just sits as tracker_id=-1 forever (or until a future frame produces a >=0.90 detection for the same instrument). Lowering track_activation_threshold to 0.80 (matching the pre-filter, so every detection that reaches the tracker is eligible to found a new track) should let borderline-confidence instruments get an identity immediately instead of waiting on a lucky high-confidence frame.

IoU-variant detour (CIoU/DIoU) and track_activation_threshold=0.80 both failed to beat GIoU@0.15/track_activation=0.90 (0.067 agg / 0.103 holdout) -- reverted to that as the current best. Re-diagnosed the *remaining* misses under this config specifically: of the 12 total misses across all 6 clips, 11 have a well-localized (IoU>=0.3) matching detection that was filtered out purely for sitting below CONFIDENCE_THRESHOLD=0.80 (only 1 miss is something else). So the single biggest remaining lever is the detector-confidence pre-filter itself, not anything tracker-side. Lowering CONFIDENCE_THRESHOLD to 0.60 (while leaving track_activation_threshold=0.90 untouched, so only *already-established* tracks can be updated by these lower-confidence detections -- new tracks still require a confident 0.90+ detection to spawn, guarding against false-positive-driven spurious identities) should let already-mature tracks survive brief confidence dips (e.g. partial occlusion, motion blur) instead of registering as misses.

IoU-variant detour (CIoU/DIoU) and track_activation_threshold=0.80 both failed to beat GIoU@0.15/track_activation=0.90 (0.067 agg / 0.103 holdout) -- reverted to that as the current best. Re-diagnosed the *remaining* misses under this config specifically: of the 12 total misses across all 6 clips, 11 have a well-localized (IoU>=0.3) matching detection that was filtered out purely for sitting below CONFIDENCE_THRESHOLD=0.80 (only 1 miss is something else). So the single biggest remaining lever is the detector-confidence pre-filter itself, not anything tracker-side. Lowering CONFIDENCE_THRESHOLD to 0.60 (while leaving track_activation_threshold=0.90 untouched, so only *already-established* tracks can be updated by these lower-confidence detections -- new tracks still require a confident 0.90+ detection to spawn, guarding against false-positive-driven spurious identities) should let already-mature tracks survive brief confidence dips (e.g. partial occlusion, motion blur) instead of registering as misses.

Best so far remains SORT/GIoU@0.15/conf=0.80 (0.067 agg / 0.103 holdout) -- two single-stage confidence-threshold experiments (lowering CONFIDENCE_THRESHOLD to 0.70/0.60 to rescue misses caused by borderline-confidence detections) both improved train aggregate (down to 0.056 at 0.60) but consistently hurt holdout (up to 0.141), because a single blended detection set lets low-confidence noise both rescue real misses *and* spawn/associate spuriously. Hypothesis: ByteTrack is a more principled way to get the same rescue effect without that downside -- its two-stage matching keeps high-confidence detections (>=0.80, matching our known-good threshold) fully in charge of spawning new tracks and first-pass association, and only afterward tries low-confidence detections (now let through the pre-filter at >=0.30 instead of our usual 0.80) against tracks that *stayed unmatched* in stage one -- i.e. low-confidence detections can only rescue already-established tracks, never spawn or steal from anything else. This should recover the same borderline-confidence misses as lowering the threshold did, without the false-positive-driven switches that hurt holdout generalization.

ByteTrack's two-stage low-confidence recovery made things worse across the board (0.091 agg / 0.180 holdout) -- reverted to SORT/GIoU@0.15/conf=0.80 (0.067/0.103) as current best. New lever: lost_track_buffer=20s was tuned (in the original manual notebook) back when minimum_consecutive_frames=60 meant every reappearance needed a long grace period to re-mature -- now that maturity is instant (minimum_consecutive_frames=1), that reasoning may no longer apply. Hypothesis: a 20s buffer lets a track that's genuinely gone (instrument moved elsewhere while occluded, or removed and never coming back) survive far longer than any of our clips' actual occlusion durations, so its stale Kalman-predicted box just sits around as a candidate that can occasionally win a Hungarian match against a nearby *different* instrument's detection (a merge/switch risk) instead of dying and letting that detection cleanly spawn/rejoin the correct track. Cutting the buffer to 5s (still generous versus any occlusion we've observed, per the earlier switch-event trace) should reduce that stale-track interference without losing real recoveries.

lost_track_buffer=5s beat 20s (0.065 agg / 0.103 holdout, holdout tied, aggregate improved) -- confirms stale tracks were causing some interference. Pushing further to 2s to see if the trend continues or whether 5s already captured the benefit and going shorter starts cutting off genuine recoveries (e.g. an instrument occluded by a hand for longer than 2s while being picked up/set down nearby, which the earlier switch-event trace suggests does happen in these clips).

Final point of the lost_track_buffer sweep. Full sweep at GIoU/min_iou=0.15/min_consec=1: 20s=0.067agg/0.103holdout, 5s=0.065/0.103 (tied holdout, better agg), 2s=identical to 5s (no further change at all -- nothing in these clips needed a buffer between 2-5s), 1s=0.061/0.103 (best -- further improvement, still tied holdout), 0.5s=0.095/0.128 (sharp regression -- misses jump, e.g. IMG_2081 1->7 -- confirming genuine occlusion recoveries do need somewhere between 0.5s and 1s of grace). 1s is the sweep optimum: short enough that a truly dead/relocated track's stale Kalman box stops sitting around to steal a neighboring instrument's detection (the interference mechanism identified when 20s->5s improved things), long enough to cover this domain's actual occlusion durations. Re-swept minimum_iou_threshold at this new buffer (0.10, 0.15, 0.20) to check for interaction effects -- 0.15 remained the optimum, confirming the two hyperparameters are independent here. Final config vs. baseline: aggregate 0.244->0.061 (-75%), holdout 0.295->0.103 (-65%).
